Psa 23:1.text: The Lord is my shepherd -- He does not say 'was' or 'will be,' but 'is.' The Psalmist speaks of a present and personal relationship with the Great Shepherd. David could truly say this because he had experienced God's care from his youth, tending his father's sheep on the hills of Bethlehem.
Psa 23:1.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 23:2.text: He maketh me to lie down in green pastures -- The Good Shepherd provides rest for His sheep. Green pastures represent spiritual refreshment and the abundant provision of grace. He does not drive us, but leads us to places of rest and nourishment.
Psa 23:2.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 23:3.text: He restoreth my soul -- When the sheep are weary, the Shepherd carries them back to the fold. So does the Lord restore our fainting spirits. He brings us back when we wander, revives us when we are cast down, and renews our strength when we are weak.
Psa 23:3.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 23:4.text: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death -- The valley is dark but it is only a shadow. Death cannot harm the sheep of Christ's flock. The Shepherd's presence turns the darkest valley into a passage of triumph. His rod and staff -- symbols of protection and guidance -- are our comfort.
Psa 23:4.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 23:5.text: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies -- The Lord is no longer described as a Shepherd but as a Host. He spreads a banquet for His children even while adversaries look on. The anointed head and overflowing cup speak of abundance and joy.
Psa 23:5.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 23:6.text: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life -- The two dogs of the Eastern shepherd are Goodness and Mercy, ever at the heels of the sheep. David is so certain of this that he says 'surely.' And the final destination is not a temporary shelter but the house of the Lord forever.
Psa 23:6.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 1:1.text: Blessed is the man -- The Psalm begins with a benediction. True happiness is found not in the counsel of the ungodly but in the law of the Lord. The blessed man is compared to a tree planted by rivers of water -- deeply rooted, constantly nourished, and bearing fruit in its season.
Psa 1:1.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 1:2.text: But his delight is in the law of the Lord -- The godly man finds pleasure, not duty, in God's Word. He meditates upon it day and night, not as a task but as a joy. Meditation is the chewing over of Scripture until its sweetness is fully extracted.
Psa 1:2.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 46:1.text: God is our refuge and strength -- In times of trouble, the believer needs no other fortress. The word 'refuge' implies a place of safety to which one runs. God is not merely a shield but a fortress, a strong tower against all danger.
Psa 46:1.author: C.H. Spurgeon

Psa 91:1.text: He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High -- The promise belongs not to the occasional visitor, but to the permanent resident. To dwell in God's presence is to abide in constant communion. The shadow of the Almighty is the coolest, safest place in all the universe.
Psa 91:1.author: C.H. Spurgeon
